additives.name: organic dyes such as eosin or rhodamine-6G

chemistry.bond_formed: C–C bonds between sp2–sp2, sp2–sp3, and sp3–sp3 hybridized carbon atoms; C–S, C–N, C–O, and C–P bonds
chemistry.scope_summary: photoredox catalysis using visible light has been applied to a large range of important organic transformations, from simple oxidations and reductions over the formation of C–C bonds between sp2–sp2, sp2–sp3, and sp3–sp3 hybridized carbon atoms to many carbon–heteroatom bond-forming reactions including C–S, C–N, C–O, and C–P bonds and cycloadditions

figures.caption: Figure 1. The old dream of using visible light for chemical reactions began with Giacomo Ciamician and Paul Silber, who ran reactions with the help of sunlight on the balcony of the Chemical Institute of the University of Bologna, Italy. Reproduced with permission of the Chemistry Department, University of Bologna, Italy. | Figure 2. Several typical photoredox catalysts with their respective absorption maxima and estimated excited-state reduction or oxidation potentials. All potentials are vs. SCE. | Figure 3. Emission spectra of four different visible-light sources used in pho- tocatalysis; intensities are normalized.

figure_descriptions: Historical perspective figure showing Ciamician and Silber
Photoredox catalyst structures with redox potentials

notes: This is a perspective paper discussing the general field of photoredox catalysis rather than describing specific batch-to-flow translations. No quantitative reaction data, optimization tables, or reactor schemes are present. The figures show historical context, catalyst structures, and light source spectra but no process diagrams.
